1. Both Supracondylar and lateral condylar fractures exhibit certain characteristic presentations, yet literature emphasizes that Supracondylar fractures particularly tend to produce marked angular deformities - a defining sign highly correlated with them.
2. The details provided outline that these angular deformities were prominently mentioned as common sequelae or expected outcomes resulting specifically from Supracondylar fractures, whereas lateral condylar fractures do indeed cause elbow deformities too but less commonly so and through varied mechanisms involving more complex considerations beyond just simple angulation.
Therefore, considering the prominence of angular deformities attributed almost exclusively to Supracondylar fractures, I concur with the initial suggestion that angular deformity seen would identify a Supracondylar Humerus Fracture rather than a Lateral Condylar Fracture where such deformity occurs much rarerly.
